text: At 2026-05-16T18:21:11, user "CORP\j.santoso" on HR-WS22.corp.local launched PowerShell with suspicious flags: -NoProfile, -WindowStyle Hidden, -EncodedCommand (base64 obfuscated payload). The parent process was Microsoft Outlook, suggesting this was triggered by a malicious email attachment or macro — a classic phishing-to-execution chain. The use of encoded commands and hidden window mode is a strong indicator of obfuscated malware execution or post-exploitation activity (T1059.001 (Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell)). At 2026-05-16T18:21:14, process "powershell.exe" initiated a TCP connection to 91.243.85.211:443. Outbound connections from PowerShell to external IPs are a hallmark of C2 communication (T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols)). At 2026-05-16T18:21:23, file "C:\Users\j.santoso\AppData\Roaming\updater.ps1" was created by "powershell.exe" (user: unknown). Dropping scripts into AppData or Temp directories is a common persistence and staging technique. At 2026-05-16T18:21:21, registry value "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\WindowsUpdateCheck" was set to "powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Users\j.santoso\AppData\Roaming\" by user "CORP\j.santoso". Modifying Run/RunOnce registry keys establishes persistence by executing the payload on every user login (T1547.001 (Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys)). At 2026-05-16T18:21:20, scheduled task "\WindowsUpdateCheck" was registered under user context "CORP\j.santoso". Creating scheduled tasks is a persistence mechanism (T1053.005 (Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task)). The sequence of events on HR-WS22.corp.local represents a complete attack chain: initial access, code execution, C2 establishment, and persistence. Immediate incident response is recommended.
